<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[af:Metaalaap (astrologie)]][[cdo:Gńēng-sń≠ng]][[de:Metall-Affe]][[en:Metal Monkey]][[fr:Singe de m√©tal]][[hak:K√Ęng-sŠĻ≥Őān]][[ja:ŚļöÁĒ≥]][[ko:Í≤Ĺžč†]][[vi:Canh Th√Ęn]][[za:Gengsaen]][[zh-min-nan:Keng-sin]][[zh-yue:ŚļöÁĒ≥]][[zh:ŚļöÁĒ≥]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[af:Metaalaap (astrologie)]][[cdo:Gńēng-sń≠ng]][[de:Metall-Affe]][[en:Metal Monkey]][[fr:Singe de m√©tal]][[hak:K√Ęng-sŠĻ≥Őān]][[ja:ŚļöÁĒ≥]][[ko:Í≤Ĺžč†]][[vi:Canh Th√Ęn]][[za:Gengsaen]][[zh-min-nan:Keng-sin]][[zh-yue:ŚļöÁĒ≥]][[zh:ŚļöÁĒ≥]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[af:Metaalaap (astrologie)]][[cdo:Gńēng-sń≠ng]][[de:Metall-Affe]][[en:Metal Monkey]][[fr:Singe de m√©tal]][[hak:K√Ęng-sŠĻ≥Őān]][[ja:ŚļöÁĒ≥]][[ko:Í≤Ĺžč†]][[vi:Canh Th√Ęn]][[za:Gengsaen]][[zh-min-nan:Keng-sin]][[zh-yue:ŚļöÁĒ≥]][[zh:ŚļöÁĒ≥]]

{{infobox Chinese|l=Yang Metal Monkey|kanji=ŚļöÁĒ≥|romaji=kanoe saru|onyomi=kŇćshin|p=gńďngshńďn|w=keng-shen|mi=k…§Ňč‚ĀĶ¬≥  ā…ôn|y=geng1 san1|j=geng1 san1|ci=k…™Ňč  ā…źn|mc=k√¶Ňč  āin|oc-bs=kň§raŇč  āin|qq=}}
{{otheruses|Sexagenary cycle|śó•śú¨šļļ„ĀģšŅ°šĽįŚĮĺŤĪ°|ŚļöÁĒ≥šŅ°šĽį|qq=}}
{{merge|1=KŇćshin|qq=}}
Might be worth moving to or merging with {{ill|KŇćshin|ja|„Āď„ĀÜ„Āó„āď|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/KŇćshin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}

{{Sexagenary cycle|qq=}}
'''Yang Metal Monkey also known as  KŇćshin''' (ŚļöÁĒ≥; „Āč„Āģ„Āą„Āē„āč„ÄĀ„Āď„ĀÜ„Āć„āď„Āģ„Āē„āč„ÄĀ„Āď„ĀÜ„Āó„āď) is one of the {{ill|Sexagenary cycle|en|Sexagenary cycle|WD=Q246658|lt=Sexagenary cycle|1=Sexagenary cycle}}.

It is the 57th combination in the Sexagenary cycle, preceded by {{ill|Kimi|ja|1=Yin Earth Goat|2=ja|Ś∑Īśú™|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kimi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and followed by {{ill|Shinyuu|ja|1=Yin Metal Rooster|2=ja|ŤĺõťÖČ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinyuu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. In {{ill|Yin-Yang Five Elements|ja|ťôįťôĹšļĒŤ°Ć|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Five Elements|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ťôįťôĹšļĒŤ°ĆśÄĚśÉ≥|1=Yin-Yang and Wuxing Theory|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11265234}}, the stem {{ill|Geng|ja|Śļö|1=Kanoe (Śļö)|lt=Kanoe (Śļö)|2=ja|3=Śļö|4=zh|5=Śļö|1=Kanoe (Śļö)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Geng|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanoe (Śļö)|qq=}} of the {{ill|Heavenly Stems|en|Heavenly Stems|WD=Q1190985|lt=Ten Heavenly Stems|1=Heavenly Stems}} represents Yang Metal, and the branch {{ill|Monkey (zodiac)|en|Monkey (zodiac)|WD=Q740674|lt=Monkey (ÁĒ≥)|1=Monkey (zodiac)}} of the {{ill|Earthly Branches|en|Earthly Branches|WD=Q1072961|lt=Twelve Earthly Branches|1=Earthly Branches}} also represents Yang Metal, a state referred to as {{ill|Bihwa|ja|šļĒŤ°ĆśÄĚśÉ≥#śĮĒŚíĆÔľą„Ā≤„āŹÔľČ|1=Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|1=Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)#Harmonious Relationship (śĮĒŚíĆ)|2=ja|3=šļĒŤ°ĆśÄĚśÉ≥#śĮĒŚíĆÔľą„Ā≤„āŹÔľČ|lt=Harmonious Relationship (Five Elements)|qq=Bihwa|lt_ja=śĮĒŚíĆ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bihwa|13=User:Immanuelle/śĮĒŚíĆ|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q218652}}.

==Pronunciation==
* English: Yang Metal Monkey (ŚļöÁĒ≥)
** Chinese (Mandarin Pinyin): gńďngshńďn
** Cantonese (Jyutping): gaang1 san1
**  Japanese:
***Kunyomi 1: Kanoe-saru
***Kunyomi 2: koukin-no-saru
***Onyomi 1: koushin
** Korean (Revised Romanization, Hangul): gyeongsin (Í≤Ĺžč†)
** Vietnamese (H√°n-ViŠĽát): Canh Th√Ęn

== Years of Yang Metal Monkey ==
{{ill|Common Era|en|Common Era|WD=Q208141|lt=Common Era|1=Common Era}} and {{ill|Japanese imperial year|en|Japanese imperial year|WD=Q1209728|lt=Imperial Era|1=Japanese imperial year}} designate years that are multiples of 60 as {{ill|Year|en|Year|WD=Q577|lt=Year|1=Year}} of KŇćshin.

=== Nature and Era Change ===
Because both the heavenly stem and earthly branch of KŇćshin are of {{ill|Metal|en|Metal|WD=Q11426|lt=Metal|1=Metal}} nature, it was believed that the metal energy pervading the universe in a KŇćshin year or day would render human hearts cold and unfeeling. The following combination, {{ill|Shinyuu|ja|1=Yin Metal Rooster|2=ja|ŤĺõťÖČ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinyuu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, also being metal in nature‚ÄĒwith Xin being of Yin energy‚ÄĒwas thought to intensify this coldness. Consequently, KŇćshin and Shinyuu were considered harbingers of political upheaval, and to avert such changes it was not uncommon to change eras for two consecutive years. For example, this occurred in the first years of {{ill|Man'en|en|Man'en|WD=Q1193862|lt=Man'en|1=Man'en}} ({{ill|1860|en|1860|WD=Q7691|lt=1860|1=1860}}) and {{ill|BunkyŇę|en|BunkyŇę|WD=Q730266|lt=Bunkyu|1=BunkyŇę}} ({{ill|1861|en|1861|WD=Q7693|lt=1861|1=1861}}).

{| class="wikitable"
|+ '''KŇćshin Years'''
|-
! {{ill|1st millennium|en|1st millennium|WD=Q25868|lt=1st Millennium|1=1st millennium}} !! {{ill|2nd millennium|en|2nd millennium|WD=Q25860|lt=2nd Millennium|1=2nd millennium}} !! {{ill|3rd millennium|en|3rd millennium|WD=Q26224|lt=3rd Millennium|1=3rd millennium}}
|-
|
* {{ill|AD 60|en|AD 60|WD=Q24927|lt=60 AD|1=AD 60}}
* {{ill|AD 120|en|AD 120|WD=Q23688|lt=120 AD|1=AD 120}}
* {{ill|180|en|180|WD=Q30884|lt=180 AD|1=180}}
* {{ill|240|en|240|WD=Q30032|lt=240 AD|1=240}}
* {{ill|300|en|300|WD=Q25641|lt=300 AD|1=300}}
* {{ill|AD 360|en|AD 360|WD=Q32458|lt=360 AD|1=AD 360}}
* {{ill|AD 420|en|AD 420|WD=Q32230|lt=420 AD|1=AD 420}}
* {{ill|480|en|480|WD=Q30329|lt=480 AD|1=480}}
* {{ill|540|en|540|WD=Q30900|lt=540 AD|1=540}}
* {{ill|600|en|600|WD=Q25586|lt=600 AD|1=600}}
* {{ill|660|en|660|WD=Q30308|lt=660 AD|1=660}}
* {{ill|720|en|720|WD=Q23539|lt=720 AD|1=720}}
* {{ill|780|en|780|WD=Q30519|lt=780 AD|1=780}}
* {{ill|840|en|840|WD=Q29936|lt=840 AD|1=840}}
* {{ill|900|en|900|WD=Q23460|lt=900 AD|1=900}}
* {{ill|960|en|960|WD=Q23705|lt=960 AD|1=960}}
|
* {{ill|1020|en|1020|WD=Q19255|lt=1020 AD|1=1020}}
* {{ill|1080|en|1080|WD=Q19448|lt=1080 AD|1=1080}}
* {{ill|1140|en|1140|WD=Q19611|lt=1140 AD|1=1140}}
* {{ill|1200|en|1200|WD=Q5224|lt=1200 AD|1=1200}}
* {{ill|1260|en|1260|WD=Q5508|lt=1260 AD|1=1260}}
* {{ill|1320|en|1320|WD=Q5768|lt=1320 AD|1=1320}}
* {{ill|1380|en|1380|WD=Q6327|lt=1380 AD|1=1380}}
* {{ill|1440|en|1440|WD=Q6384|lt=1440 AD|1=1440}}
* {{ill|1500|en|1500|WD=Q6126|lt=1500 AD|1=1500}}
* {{ill|1560|en|1560|WD=Q6613|lt=1560 AD|1=1560}}
* {{ill|1620|en|1620|WD=Q6810|lt=1620 AD|1=1620}}
* {{ill|1680|en|1680|WD=Q7642|lt=1680 AD|1=1680}}
* {{ill|1740|en|1740|WD=Q7006|lt=1740 AD|1=1740}}
* {{ill|1800|en|1800|WD=Q6875|lt=1800 AD|1=1800}}
* {{ill|1860|en|1860|WD=Q7691|lt=1860 AD|1=1860}}
* {{ill|1920|en|1920|WD=Q2155|lt=1920 AD|1=1920}}
* {{ill|1980|en|1980|WD=Q2439|lt=1980 AD|1=1980}}
|
* {{ill|2040 AD|ja|2040ŚĻī|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/2040 AD|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=2040|WD=Q12108}}
* {{ill|2100 AD|ja|2100ŚĻī|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/2100 AD|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 2100ŚĻīšĽ£|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=2100s|WD=Q1209304}}
* {{ill|2160 AD|ja|2160ŚĻī|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/2160 AD|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 22šłĖÁīÄ|1=22nd century|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11177}}
* {{ill|2220 AD|ja|2220ŚĻī|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/2220 AD|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 23šłĖÁīÄ|1=23rd century|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q49832}}
* {{ill|2280 AD|ja|2280ŚĻī|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/2280 AD|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 23šłĖÁīÄ|1=23rd century|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q49832}}
* {{ill|2340 AD|ja|2340ŚĻī|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/2340 AD|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 24šłĖÁīÄ|1=24th century|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q49838}}
* {{ill|2400 AD|ja|2400ŚĻī|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/2400 AD|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 24šłĖÁīÄ|1=24th century|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q49838}}
* {{ill|2460 AD|ja|2460ŚĻī|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/2460 AD|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|2520 AD|ja|2520ŚĻī|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/2520 AD|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 3ŚćÉŚĻīÁīÄ|1=3rd millennium|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q26224}}
* {{ill|2580 AD|ja|2580ŚĻī|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/2580 AD|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|2640 AD|ja|2640ŚĻī|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/2640 AD|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|2700 AD|ja|2700ŚĻī|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/2700 AD|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|2760 AD|ja|2760ŚĻī|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/2760 AD|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|2820 AD|ja|2820ŚĻī|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/2820 AD|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|2880 AD|ja|2880ŚĻī|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/2880 AD|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|2940 AD|ja|2940ŚĻī|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/2940 AD|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|3000 AD|ja|3000ŚĻī|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/3000 AD|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 3ŚćÉŚĻīÁīÄ|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=3rd millennium|WD=Q26224}}
|}

== Months of Yang Metal Monkey ==
In years where the last digit of the Western Calendar year is 3 or 8 (when the heavenly stem is {{ill|Gu«ź|ja|Áôł|1=Mizunoto (Áôł)|lt=Mizunoto (Áôł)|2=ja|3=Áôł|4=zh|5=Áôł|1=Mizunoto (Áôł)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gu«ź|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizunoto (Áôł)|qq=}} or {{ill|Wu|ja|śąä|1=Tsuchinoe (śąä)|lt=Tsuchinoe (śąä)|2=ja|3=śąä|4=zh|5=śąä|1=Tsuchinoe (śąä)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wu|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchinoe (śąä)|qq=}}), the month of {{ill|July|en|July|WD=Q121|lt=July|1=July}} becomes the KŇćshin {{ill|Month|en|Month|WD=Q5151|lt=Month (Calendar)|1=Month}}. Note that here the month may refer either to a month of the {{ill|Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|ja|śóßśö¶|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=}} or to a {{ill|Solar term|en|Solar term|WD=Q1190993|lt=Solar term|1=Solar term}} (from {{ill|Liqiu|en|Liqiu|WD=Q1147421|lt=Start of Autumn|1=Liqiu}} until the day before {{ill|Bailu (solar term)|en|Bailu (solar term)|WD=Q1191003|lt=White Dew|1=Bailu (solar term)}}).

== Days of Yang Metal Monkey ==

On KŇćshin days, a KŇćshin Vigil ({{ill|KŇćshin Vigil|ja|ŚļöÁĒ≥ŚĺÖ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/KŇćshin Vigil|1=KŇćshin-machi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11486004}}, also known as  {{ill|KŇćshin Lecture|ja|ŚļöÁĒ≥Ť¨õ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/KŇćshin Lecture|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ŚļöÁĒ≥ŚĺÖ|1=KŇćshin-machi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11486004}}) was held. {{main|{{ill|KŇćshin|en|KŇćshin|WD=Q3082157|lt=KŇćshin Faith|1=KŇćshin}}|qq=}}

A KŇćshin day is also the {{ill|Ennichi|en|Ennichi|WD=Q985655|lt=Festival Day|1=Ennichi}} of {{ill|Ňöakra (Buddhism)|en|Ňöakra (Buddhism)|WD=Q1444745|lt=Taishakuten|1=Ňöakra (Buddhism)}}.

=== Choosing a Day ===
KŇćshin days occur on the sixth day (excluding intercalary days {{ill|Intercalary Day|ja|ťĖďśó•|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Intercalary Day|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) of the {{ill|Hassen|ja|ŚÖęŚįā|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hassen|qq=}} cycle.
== See Also ==
* {{prefix|qq=}}
* {{intitle|qq=}}

== External Links ==
* {{Kotobank|2=„ÉĖ„É™„āŅ„Éč„āęŚõĹťöõŚ§ßÁôĺÁßĎšļčŚÖł ŚįŹť†ÖÁõģšļčŚÖł|qq=}}

{{Short description|Folk religion|qq=}}
{{distinguish|Ko-ShintŇć|qq=}}
{{more footnotes|date=August 2020|qq=}}
{{Italic title|reason=|qq=}}

{{nihongo|'''''KŇćshin'''''|ŚļöÁĒ≥|1=KŇćshin|lt=Yang Metal Monkey|2=ja|3=ŚļöÁĒ≥|4=zh|5=ŚļöÁĒ≥|6=ko|7=Í≤Ĺžč†|lt_ko=Í≤Ĺžč†|8=fr|9=Singe de m√©tal|10=de|11=Metall-Affe||qq=}} or {{nihongo|''KŇćshin-shinkŇć''|ŚļöÁĒ≥šŅ°šĽį||qq=}} is a folk belief in {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} with [[Taoism|Taoist]] origins, influenced by [[Shinto]], {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhism|1=Buddhism}} and other local beliefs.

An event related to the belief is called {{nihongo|KŇćshin-kŇć|ŚļöÁĒ≥Ť¨õ||qq=}}, held on the KŇćshin days that occur every 60 days in accordance with the [[:en:Chinese sexagenary cycle|Chinese sexagenary cycle]]. On this day some believers stay awake to prevent {{nihongo|Sanshi|šłČŚįł||qq=}}, entities believed to live inside the bodies of believers, from leaving during that night in order to report the deeds of believers to the god Tentei.

It is not known when this belief arrived in Japan, but it was widespread among the nobles of the imperial court by some time in the 9th century. The Japanese monk {{ill|Ennin|en|Ennin|WD=Q732282|lt=Ennin|1=Ennin}}, visiting {{ill|Tang dynasty|en|Tang dynasty|WD=Q9683|lt=Tang China|1=Tang dynasty}} in 838, wrote that "This night no one sleeps. It is the same as New Year's Eve and the nights of ''KŇćshin'' in our country."<ref>{{Cite book |last= |first= |url=http://archive.org/details/diaryrecordofpil0000enni |title=Ennin's Diary. The Record of a Pilgrimage to China in Search of the Law |title-link= |publisher=Ronald Press Company |year=1955 |location=New York |page=58 |translator-last=Reischauer |translator-first=Edwin O. |translator-link=Edwin O. Reischauer|qq=}}</ref> In the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}, {{ill|Bhikkhu|en|Bhikkhu|WD=Q854997|lt=Buddhist monks|1=Bhikkhu}} started to write about KŇćshin and spread the belief to a wider public. Numerous monuments or pillars called {{nihongo|KŇćshin-tŇć|ŚļöÁĒ≥Ś°Ē||qq=}} (or also {{nihongo|KŇćshin-zuka|ŚļöÁĒ≥Ś°ö||qq=}}) were erected all over the country, and the belief remained very popular during the  {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}. However, after the Meiji government issued the [[Shinbutsu bunri|Shinto and Buddhism Separation Order]] in 1872, KŇćshin and other folk beliefs were officially rejected as superstitions.

Belief in KŇćshin survives, although it is far less popular than it once was. Many KŇćshin-tŇć have been moved inside Buddhist temples or private homes, but  many remain alongside roads and there are still some well-maintained {{nihongo|KŇćshin-dŇć|KŇćshin-dŇć||qq=}} (KŇćshin halls) either attached to Buddhist temples or standing alone.

==Deities and customs==
The most ancient KŇćshin custom is that of staying awake one special night every sixty days. This night is called ''KŇćshin-Machi'' (ŚļöÁĒ≥ŚĺÖ - KŇćshin Waiting).

The main KŇćshin belief is the concept that {{ill|Three Corpses|en|Three Corpses|WD=Q9380693|lt=Three Corpses|1=Three Corpses}}, or ''Sanshi'' (šłČŚįł), live in every human body. The Sanshi keep track of the deeds of the person they inhabit. On the night called KŇćshin-Machi, the ''Sanshis'' leave the body and go to [[:en:King of Heavens|Tentei]] (Ś§©ŚłĚ), the Heavenly God, to report the deeds of that person. Tentei then punishes bad people, making them ill, shortening their lifespans or, in extreme cases, ending their lives. Believers in KŇćshin try to live without performing bad deeds, but those who believe that they have reason to fear try to stay awake during KŇćshin nights, as the only way to prevent the Sanshi from reporting to Tentei.

In the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, KŇćshin-Machi became more popular among commoners, leading to a proliferation of festivities and cultic activities. Many of these practices centered on the worship of specific deities. These deities were visualized in {{ill|Hanging scroll|en|Hanging scroll|WD=Q2188827|lt=Hanging scroll|1=Hanging scroll}}s, icons, and stone carvings, many of which continue to dot the rural landscape of Japan today. One specially prominent god in the KŇćshin cult was [[ShŇćmen-KongŇć]] (Blue-Faced [[:en:Vajra-YakŠĻ£a|Vajra-YakŠĻ£a]]), a fearsome deity with many arms. The first scriptures that established his connection with KŇćshin were produced in the context of esoteric Buddhist rituals performed at the temple known as {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:OnjŇćji|OnjŇćji]]|qq=}} or {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Miidera|Miidera]]|qq=}}. Another temple crucial in the early development of this cult was [[ShitennŇć-ji]]. ShŇćmen-KongŇć became KŇćshin-san when people expected this deity to make the Sanshis themselves ill and prevent them from going to Tentei.

==See also==
* {{ill|Yasaka KŇćshin-dŇć|en|Yasaka KŇćshin-dŇć|WD=Q3571897|lt=Yasaka KŇćshin-dŇć|1=Yasaka KŇćshin-dŇć}}

==References and further reading==
{{reflist|qq=}}
* Emiko Ohnuki-Tierney, ''The Monkey as Mirror: Symbolic Transformations in Japanese History and Ritual (Asian Studies/Anthropology)'',  Princeton University Press, 1989, {{ISBN|978-0-691-02846-0|qq=}}
* Kubo, Noritada (Á™™ Śĺ≥ŚŅ†) ''KŇćshin ShinkŇć no KenkyŇę: NitchŇę ShŇękyŇć Bunka KŇćshŇć-shi (ŚļöÁĒ≥šŅ°šĽį„ĀģÁ†ĒÁ©∂‚Äēśó•šł≠ŚģóśēôśĖáŚĆĖšļ§śłČŚŹ≤)'' (Research on Belief in KŇćshin - A History of Cultural-Religious Exchange,  Nihon  Gakujutsu  ShinkŇć-kai, 1961.
* Emiko Ohnuki-Tierney, ''Illness and Culture in Contemporary Japan: An Anthropological View'', Cambridge University Press, 1984, {{ISBN|978-0-521-27786-0|qq=}}
* Lafcadio Hearn, ''Japan's Religions: Shinto and Buddhism'', Kessinger Publishing Co, 2003, {{ISBN|978-0-7661-7657-7|qq=}} 
* Ichiro Hori, ''Folk Religion in Japan: Continuity and Change'', University of Chicago Press, 1974, {{ISBN|978-0-226-35334-0|qq=}}
* Livia Kohn, ''Daoism and Chinese Culture'', Three Pines Press, 2005, {{ISBN|978-1-931483-00-1|qq=}}
* {{ill|Richard Bowring|en|Richard Bowring|WD=Q7324287|lt=Richard Bowring|1=Richard Bowring}}, ''The Religious Traditions of Japan'',  Cambridge University Press, 2005, {{ISBN|978-0-521-85119-0|qq=}}
* [http://www.three-monkeys.info/1/ENG.htm  Three-Monkeys site with related research and information]

== External links ==
*[https://web.archive.org/web/20120316111118/http://www.irohaproject.org/blog/2010/03/09/koushin-1/ The koshin belief, The Iroha Project]

{{Shinto shrine|qq=}}

{{Hiyoshi Faith|qq=}}

[[zh-min-nan:Kap-ch√ļ]]
[[zh-yue:ÁĒ≤Śćą]]
[[File:koshinscroll.jpg|thumb|A KŇćshin scroll|right]]

{{translated page|ja|ŚļöÁĒ≥šŅ°šĽį|version=102438991|comment=Imported full ja history}}



[[cdo:G√°k-cŠĻ≥ŐĄde]]
[[nds:Holt-Pferdevi]]
[[de:KŇćshin]]

[[en:KŇćshin]]
[[fa:ŕ©ŔąōīŘĆŔÜ ōīŘĆŔÜŕ©Ŕą]]
[[zh:ŚļöÁĒ≥šŅ°šĽį]]
[[ja:ŚļöÁĒ≥šŅ°šĽį]]
[[ru:–ö–ĺ—Ā–ł–Ĺ]]
[[fr:KŇćshin]]
[[ja:ŚļöÁĒ≥]]
[[zh:ŚļöÁĒ≥]]
[[ko:Í≤Ĺžč†]]
[[fr:Singe de m√©tal]]
[[de:Metall-Affe]]

[[af:Houtperd (astrologie)]]
[[de:Holz-Pferdeen]]
[[en:Wood Horse]]
[[fr:Cheval de boisboishak]]
[[it:Kap-chŠĻ≥ŐĀid]]

[[ko:ÍįĎžė§]]
[[vi:Gi√°p NgŠĽć]]
[[za:Gyapceij]]
[[zh:ÁĒ≤Śćą]]

[[ar:KŇćshin]]

<!--please transliterate all the hiragana readings into romaji and provide the kanji--><!--please transliterate all the hiragana readings into romaji and provide the kanji--><!--KŇćshin-11| ]]

<!--KŇćshin--><!--KŇćshin-->

<!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis-->
<!--zhwiki derived wikidata interwikis-->
<!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis-->
{{wikidata link|Q798775}}
[[Category:Japanese folk religion]]
[[Category:Japanese words and phrases]]
[[Category:KŇćshin]]
[[Category:KŇćshin| ]]
[[Category:Regions of Asia]]
[[Category:Regions of Japan]]
[[Category:Religious towers]]
[[Category:SannŇć Ichijitsu ShintŇć]]
[[Category:Sexagenary cycle members]]
[[Category:Git synced pages]]
[[Category:Shinbutsu shŇęgŇć]]
[[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]]
[[Category:Wikidata has short description]]
[[Category:WikiProject Japanese Calendar]]
